video.transcript_text: Handloom fabrics and their ornamentation in India is an age-old traditional technique. The Indian traditional handloom holds supreme among the beautiful costumes of the world. Its origin is lost in antiquity. A lot of times, consumers find it difficult to find authentic handloom. Though there is an increasing demand for handicrafts and handloom products, there is a dearth of authenticity in fabric and also the dearth of authentic sources to get this fabric from. It would be everyone's dream also to design their own clothing either because of the interest or because of the unavailability of exactly suited colours or designs in the market. Handloom is a very big industry in India with a negligible market. India has a land of artistic weavers who master the art of creating beautiful handmade clothing. There are n number of weavers or artisans across India but with exponential multiplication of automated textile industries it is becoming more and more difficult for these weavers to also earn their daily bread. So Alika here aims to create a platform for customers to access authentic handloom directly from the weavers themselves. It also offers pallets for customization which means a customer can select each bead and thread that they want their saree to be weaved with. This pallet would be generated based on what weaver could offer. This would be the blueprint for the weaver miles away. Customers could access Alika's digital page like website or app to create their own customised handloom master piece. As a customer lands on Alika's home page they would get to select the type of handloom they are looking for. For example Uppada, Mangalgiri, Gadwal, Chinderi where they would get description of each of these types. For example Uppada and its importance and the process of how Uppada is made and the names and pictures of weavers and they can proceed to the design page. In the design page, customer gets to select the colour of the thread, the weaving pattern either blocks or lines and the designs that they could offer like lotuses, diamonds, mango leaves, anything or if there are any accessories. And when customer is done with the dream design they would be navigated to a registration page and then through the payment kit. In the end customer's dashboard would be created. So this dashboard they can monitor the progress of their weaving and as the cloth takes a particular shape a picture of the weaver weaving their master piece would also be sent to the customer.

video.transcript_text: Hi, I'm Will, and we're luminous. We're developing a low-cost blood test to screen for many types of cancer. Now we can detect cancer early in stage one, and that's important because early detection will save millions of lives. Many people have tried to develop blood tests to screen for cancer, but it's tough because blood is very complex. This is just a handful of the 40,000 proteins and countless tumor genes that can be found in blood. And the old approach of searching for one or a few of these markers to diagnose disease doesn't work. It's like reading three pages of a novel and expecting to understand the entire plot. So we've invented a much more comprehensive approach. We use lasers to analyze your blood, and this is called Raman spectroscopy. It prepares for us a snapshot of many different molecules in your blood rather than just one or a few. And we can then use machine learning to find patterns that allow us to diagnose disease. And this means from one blood sample, we can screen for many types of cancer simultaneously. Now the cost to perform our test is less than $5, and this is compared to the hundreds or thousands in dollars that it costs to perform genomic space tests. And we've built our prototype and tested it on 200 blood samples from top cancer centers. And our early results for breast cancer screening show over 87% accuracy. And this is only increasing as we scale up our data set. We have the right team to develop this technology fast. My co-founders Storm and Samantha have over 14 years experience with Raman spectroscopy while Storm and I have managed multinational clinical trials. And we have patients enrolling in our pilot studies now. This puts us on track to be on the market by 2020. Now where there's a huge need, there's also a huge opportunity. There are over 120 million Americans of typical screening age, and with a test even in the low hundreds of dollars, this is a $20 billion market. So again, we're luminous, we're detecting cancer early, and if you share our passion for this problem, come speak with us. Thanks. Thank you.

title: Molecular Reality YC Demo Day (S18) - Biology is Just Beginning [OhqNaEx7ilU]

video.file_name: C:\Users\aravi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\Startup-Pitch-Evaluation\backend\outputs\batch_input\Molecular Reality YC Demo Day (S18) - Biology is Just Beginning [OhqNaEx7ilU].mkv
video.transcript_text: Hi, my name is Kent. I'm the CEO and founder of DemonPore. We built the world's first mechanical nanopores. Nanopores are used in scientific instruments to identify DNA molecules, or I'm sorry, to identify molecules, like DNA. So this is how they work. You pass your molecule through a tiny hole, the nanopore. As it passes through, it generates an electrical signal, and that electrical signal is part of a unique fingerprint that lets you identify the molecule. There's been an explosion of interest in this field recently, and here are just three examples of companies to highlight that interest. But all of these companies are making static nanopores, which just means that the size of the hole is fixed. Because of that, they can only easily look at molecules that are a good fit for the poor. So in practice, most of them can only look at DNA molecules. We're taking this technology to the next level. We invented the world's first mechanical nanopore, which means we can change its size, which means that we can look at pretty much any kind of molecule. In the future, one DemonPore instrument will be able to look at viruses, proteins, toxins, metabolites, any biomarker you can think of, virtually any molecule at the nanometer scale. This is our instrument, and today, for the first time in the world, here at Demo Day, we're going to show a mechanical nanopore. And here is an actual mechanical nanopore inside of the instrument, and we're adjusting this with nanometer sensitivity by sliding two atomically flat, atomically sharp surfaces by each other, which creates this esosceles equilateral triangle that is the DemonPore. This might look simple, and it is a simple idea, but there was a huge amount of expertise and development that went into this accomplishment. I was able to do this with 10 years of experience in this field. I helped found Halcyon Molecular, a pioneering genome technology startup that raised $20 million. I led the chemistry group, and I recruited an amazing team of more than 70 scientists and engineers. We're DemonPore. We built the world's first mechanical nanopores, which is a crucial step on the path to a holy grail of biotechnology, a universal biomolecular sensor. Thanks.
